description: Which hardware and software products fall within the regulation's mandatory requirements, including debates over open-source software, SaaS, and bespoke enterprise solutions.
salience: Tech industry lobbies hard to narrow scope and exclude open-source/SaaS; civil society and ENISA push for broad coverage to close gaps; SMEs fear disproportionate compliance burden.

description: The stringency and prescriptiveness of the mandatory security-by-design and security-by-default technical requirements manufacturers must meet.
salience: Manufacturers want flexible, principles-based requirements; security researchers and consumer groups demand concrete, enforceable standards; tension between innovation and prescriptive mandates.

description: How long manufacturers must provide security updates and support for products, and what obligations apply at end-of-life.
salience: Consumer and civil society groups demand long support periods; hardware manufacturers resist open-ended obligations; debate over who bears cost when products outlive commercial support windows.

description: Whether self-declaration of conformity is sufficient or mandatory third-party assessment by notified bodies is required, and for which product risk classes.
salience: Industry prefers self-declaration to reduce cost and speed time-to-market; regulators and security experts push for mandatory third-party assessment for high-risk products; notified bodies lobby for expanded mandates.

description: Mandatory timelines and mechanisms for reporting actively exploited vulnerabilities and security incidents to ENISA and market surveillance authorities.
salience: Security researchers and vendors clash over mandatory 72-hour reporting windows that may force premature disclosure before patches are ready; intelligence agencies concerned about mandatory reporting creating exploitable intelligence; tension with NIS2 reporting obligations.

description: Whether and how open-source software projects and their stewards bear obligations under the regulation, including exemptions for non-commercial development.
salience: Open-source foundations and community developers fear being liable for widely-used projects they maintain voluntarily; commercial vendors that build on OSS want clarity on who is responsible; Commission seeks not to chill open-source innovation while closing supply-chain gaps.

description: How market surveillance authorities across Member States coordinate enforcement, including powers to withdraw non-compliant products and impose penalties.
salience: Member States with lighter regulatory traditions resist strong Commission oversight; industry warns of fragmented enforcement creating unlevel playing field; debate over penalty caps (up to €15M or 2.5% global turnover) and their proportionality for SMEs.

description: Whether the regulation's obligations are proportionate for small and medium-sized enterprises and micro-enterprises, and what targeted relief or phase-in periods apply.
salience: SME associations and startup lobbies argue compliance costs are existential for small players; consumer groups counter that exemptions create dangerous loopholes; Commission seeks balance between innovation and security floors.

description: Which technical standards satisfy the essential requirements, who develops them, and the role of European Standardisation Organisations versus international bodies.
salience: Industry wants to use existing international standards (ISO/IEC) rather than bespoke EU standards; ESOs and Commission want EU standards to reflect European values and policy goals; debate over presumption of conformity timelines.

description: The extent to which manufacturers must assess and take responsibility for the cybersecurity of third-party components and upstream software dependencies.
salience: Large integrators fear liability for vulnerabilities in components they did not develop; component vendors resist obligations flowing down; security community and government users demand SBOMs for critical infrastructure procurement decisions.

description: How the Cyber Resilience Act interacts with and avoids duplication or conflict with sector-specific regulations such as NIS2, the AI Act, the Radio Equipment Directive, and the Medical Devices Regulation.
salience: Regulated industries (health, automotive, aviation) lobby to stay under their existing sectoral regimes; Commission faces political pressure to ensure coherence; lex specialis carve-outs create fragmentation risks.
